themes.title: Love Tokens
themes.description: Plants traditionally given as symbols of love and devotion.
themes.intro: Roses, peonies, and forget-me-nots — for thousands of years, gardeners have grown specific plants to say what mouths cannot. This collection gathers the classic love-tokens of Western and Eastern traditions, from Persian rose to Indian jasmine.

plants.reason: the classical "rose of roses," source of rose attar and centerpiece of romantic symbolism since ancient Persia.
the ancestor of nearly all modern roses, grown in monastery gardens as "the rose of love."
yellow-pink blend launched at the 1945 UN founding; the most iconic 20th-century love rose.
Napoleon's signature flower, worn as a token of devotion to Josephine.
Mother's Day flower and classic love letter enclosure; pink and red are romantic.
red tulips symbolize true love in Turkish and Persian poetry.
woven into bridal garlands across South and Southeast Asia.
bridal flower symbolizing prosperity and romance in Chinese tradition.
the "bonds of love" of Victorian flower language, entwining stems.
universal symbol of remembrance between lovers parted.

themes.title: Broken Hearts
themes.description: Flowers of grief, longing, and lost love.
themes.intro: Some flowers exist to mark loss — cyclamen for goodbye, lily for the funeral, poppy for the war dead, forget-me-not for the departed. This collection holds the plants people grow when love has slipped or someone has gone.

plants.reason: the flower of Narcissus himself, myth of self-love turned to sorrow.
born from the blood of Apollo's slain lover Hyacinthos.
Greek myth of Adonis's blood; bloom of fleeting love.
Victorian flower language for resignation and goodbye.
the remembrance flower of Flanders Fields.
worn by Kaiser Wilhelm I in memory of his mother; symbol of single blessedness.
the winter-bloomer of melancholy and hidden tears.
Victorian "gallantry," often given to break off engagements.
funeral flower of innocence and resurrection.
the classic keepsake of the departed.

themes.title: Wedding Classics
themes.description: Plants historically used in Western wedding bouquets and altars.
themes.intro: Western wedding traditions use a small bridal repertoire — roses for romance, peonies for prosperity, lily of the valley for purity. Many were sealed by royal weddings and have stayed in florist catalogs ever since.

plants.reason: the 20th-century wedding rose par excellence.
June wedding classic; 12th anniversary flower.
the traditional bridal crown flower.
Victorian corsage flower carrying "secret love."
purity and virginity; Catholic bridal traditions.
Kate Middleton's 2011 royal wedding bouquet.
modern lush wedding filler in whites and blues.
forgiveness and worthiness in bridal symbolism.
boutonniere standard for grooms since the 19th century.
Hindu, Filipino, and Indonesian wedding strands.

themes.title: Secret Language
themes.description: Victorian floriography — each plant conveys a coded message.
themes.intro: In Victorian England, every garden plant carried a coded meaning. Heartsease said 'think of me'; mignonette said 'your qualities surpass your charms.' This collection rebuilds a small sample of that floriographic vocabulary for letters that cannot be plain.

plants.reason: "think of me"; carried between separated lovers.
modesty and faithfulness.
gallantry.
"Thank you."
affection but also fleeting love (blooms last a day).
preference; "you are my choice."
true love and memory.
the "bonds of love."
"your qualities surpass your charms."
return of happiness.

themes.title: Famous Legends
themes.description: Plants born of myth and legend.
themes.intro: Plants as the source material of myth — narcissus born from a self-loving boy, laurel born from a fleeing nymph, lotus that carried Egyptian souls into the sun. These ten species have lived as long in story as in soil.

plants.reason: Greek myth of Narcissus, who fell in love with his reflection.
created by Apollo from the blood of Hyacinthos.
Daphne transformed into laurel to escape Apollo; crown of Olympic victors.
flower of Morpheus and Demeter in Greek myth.
said to spring from the tears of Aphrodite over Adonis.
Buddhist symbol of enlightenment; Egyptian rebirth.
living fossil, survived Hiroshima atomic bomb.
medieval legend of a shepherd girl's tear at the Nativity.
Shakespeare's "love-in-idleness" from A Midsummer Night's Dream.
Celtic tree of immortality; planted in churchyards for 1000+ years.

themes.title: Royal Favorites
themes.description: Plants named for or beloved by royalty.
themes.intro: Plants named for or beloved by royalty — Strelitzia for Queen Charlotte, Victoria amazonica for Queen Victoria, the Apothecary's Rose of the House of Lancaster, Sultan Ahmed's moonlight tulips. Each carries a small flag of empire pressed into its petals.

plants.reason: named in 1773 for Queen Charlotte, Duchess of Mecklenburg-Strelitz.
presented to every delegate at the 1945 UN founding.
named for Queen Victoria; her gardeners grew it at Chatsworth.
compact succulent named in her honor.
1954 Grandiflora named for the young queen.
imperial flower of the Tang dynasty court.
Ottoman Sultan Ahmed III staged moonlight Tulip Festivals.
Kate Middleton and Grace Kelly's royal bouquets.
Napoleon's Empress Josephine grew vast collections at Malmaison.
the "Red Rose of Lancaster" from the Wars of the Roses.

themes.title: Explorer's Prize
themes.description: Introduced to Western gardens by plant hunters of the 18th–20th centuries.
themes.intro: Plant hunters of the 18th-20th centuries crossed Himalayan passes and Yunnan ravines for species European gardeners had never seen. E.H. Wilson nearly died for the Dove Tree. Every plant here changed Western horticulture the moment it arrived.

plants.reason: E.H. Wilson's 1904 China expedition prize, nearly cost him his life.
one of Augustine Henry's Yunnan finds.
discovered by Frank Kingdon-Ward in Tibet, 1924.
thought extinct for 50M years; rediscovered in China 1944.
named by Joseph Hooker in the Himalayas, 1855.
Joseph Rock's 1925 Tibetan find; world's most expensive peony.
E.H. Wilson's 1903 Sichuan collection for Veitch.
Italian missionary Giuseppe Giraldi's 1890s Shaanxi find.
Wallich's 1824 Himalayan collection.
Francis Masson's 1773 Kew collection from South Africa.

themes.title: Pioneer Plants
themes.description: Essential crops and landscape plants of American frontier life.
themes.intro: American frontier life ran on a small toolkit — sunflower oil, pumpkin pies, weeping willow shade, Osage Orange windbreaks. The Three Sisters of Indigenous agriculture fed colonial settlers and shaped the landscape from Ohio to the Great Plains.

plants.reason: Indigenous staple carried west; oil, seed, and livestock fodder.
Indigenous root crop revived by 19th-century settlers.
half of the "Three Sisters" along with corn and squash.
settler staple for pies, stock feed, and jack-o-lanterns.
prairie wildflower rapidly colonized roadsides behind settlers.
planted by USDA 1930s as livestock fencing; now invasive.
Johnny Appleseed's (John Chapman) Ohio frontier apple orchards.
Southern homestead staple; survives poor soil.
the original windbreak hedge of the Great Plains.
planted at homestead wells for shade and basketry withes.

themes.title: Revolutionary Tea
themes.description: Colonial-era Chinese-tea substitutes during the Boston Tea Party boycott.
themes.intro: When Boston colonists threw the East India Company's tea into the harbor, they had to brew something else. Bee Balm became Oswego tea, mint and sage filled patriotic kettles, and every kitchen kept a homestead alternative.

plants.reason: "Oswego tea" of the Iroquois and New York settlers.
leaves steeped as tea across the eastern frontier.
leaves were the most-brewed substitute during the Revolution.
leaves made a serviceable tea in colonial kitchens.
hips yielded a tart vitamin-C-rich tea and jelly.
settler garden standby; easy to grow, easy to brew.
18th-century hybrid rapidly naturalized in American kitchens.
soothing bedtime brew carried in every homestead.
"sage tea" ubiquitous for sore throats and general wellness.
flower heads brewed as a bitter herbal tonic tea.

themes.title: Literary Icons
themes.description: Plants made famous in classic literature.
themes.intro: Plants made famous by writers — Wordsworth's daffodils, Shakespeare's heartsease, Ophelia's rosemary for remembrance, Tennyson's lotos-eaters, Housman's loveliest of trees. Each species here carries a fingerprint of English literature pressed into its leaves.

plants.reason: Shakespeare's "love-in-idleness" in A Midsummer Night's Dream.
"for remembrance" — Ophelia, Hamlet.
the rose of Shakespeare's sonnets.
Wordsworth's "host of golden daffodils."
The Wizard of Oz poppy field.
Tennyson's "Lotos-Eaters."
Housman's "Loveliest of Trees."
biblical lily of the valley and medieval Marian poetry.
"bring back, bring back the Bluebells" — folk song; ruin-haunted poetry.
the graveyard tree in Gray's "Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard."

themes.title: Ancient Medicine
themes.description: Plants that defined pre-modern pharmacology.
themes.intro: For 6000 years, before laboratories synthesized molecules, healers walked into gardens. Foxglove for the heart, willow bark for fever, aloe for burns, ginkgo for memory. This collection holds the plants that defined pre-modern pharmacology.

plants.reason: source of digoxin, mainstay of heart failure treatment since 1785 (Withering).
bark source of salicin, precursor to aspirin.
source of paclitaxel (Taxol), foundational cancer chemotherapy.
6000-year burn treatment, from Egyptian papyri to modern pharmacies.
traditional Chinese medicine for circulation; modern memory supplement.
medieval and modern herbal antidepressant.
source of thujone; antiparasitic since antiquity.
menthol — cooling agent from Pharaonic Egypt onward.
Hippocrates' "medicine chest"; modern flu remedy.
thymol — antimicrobial used on Greek and Roman battlefields.

themes.title: Native Remedies
themes.description: North American Indigenous medicinal plants.
themes.intro: Echinacea for snakebite, butterfly weed for pleurisy, joe pye weed for typhus — North American Indigenous herbalists assembled a sophisticated pharmacopoeia from continent-spanning plant knowledge later absorbed into modern American herbalism with the tribes uncredited.

plants.reason: Plains tribes' universal toothache, snakebite, and wound remedy.
Blackfoot and Ojibwa antiseptic and fever medicine.
"pleurisy root," Cherokee respiratory medicine.
Indigenous respiratory and emetic medicine (not in library; use Lobelia cardinalis).
Iroquois ceremonial and medicinal plant.
tuber staple of Pacific Northwest tribes.
Indigenous smoking herb and urinary medicine.
wound-staunch herb ("soldier's woundwort") of many tribes.
Mohican herbalist Joseph Shauquethqueat's typhus cure.
Ojibwa earache drops and cold remedy.

themes.title: Modern Drugs
themes.description: Plants that directly produced pharmaceutical medicines.
themes.intro: Some of medicine's most powerful drugs are still sourced from plants. Foxglove gives us digoxin, English yew gives us Taxol cancer chemo, Sweet Wormwood gives us artemisinin (2015 Nobel). The pharmacy still grows in the garden.

plants.reason: digoxin/digitoxin, standard heart failure drug.
paclitaxel (Taxol), first-line chemotherapy for breast and ovarian cancer.
10-DAB precursor for semi-synthetic paclitaxel manufacture.
salicin → modern aspirin.
artemisinin, the WHO-standard antimalarial (2015 Nobel).
vincristine and vinblastine, leukemia chemotherapy.
morphine, codeine, and all modern opioid analgesics.
atropine, used in cardiac arrest and ophthalmology.
etoposide, testicular and lung cancer chemotherapy.
ephedrine, bronchodilator and decongestant.

themes.title: Healing Herbs
themes.description: Everyday home-apothecary herbs for minor ailments.
themes.intro: The herbs you keep on a kitchen sill or bedside pot for everyday minor ailments — chamomile for sleep, peppermint for headache, lavender for anxiety, calendula for cuts, echinacea for colds. Folk apothecaries across centuries.

plants.reason: gel for burns, sunburn, and minor skin irritation.
sleep and digestive tea.
indigestion, headache, and nausea.
sore throat gargle and oral hygiene.
cough suppressant and antimicrobial.
anxiety, insomnia, and burns.
calming tea for stress and sleep.
topical salve for cuts and chapped skin.
immune support during colds.
memory, circulation, and tonic hair rinse.

themes.title: Air Purifiers
themes.description: From the 1989 NASA Clean Air Study and subsequent research.
themes.intro: In 1989, NASA tested houseplants in sealed chambers and found that several common species genuinely scrub formaldehyde, benzene, and trichloroethylene from the air. This collection pulls together the NASA Clean Air winners that earn their keep.

plants.reason: formaldehyde, benzene, and xylene removal; nearly indestructible.
removes ammonia, benzene, formaldehyde, and trichloroethylene.
rare CAM photosynthesis, releases oxygen at night.
formaldehyde and xylene; non-toxic to pets.
xylene, trichloroethylene, formaldehyde.
formaldehyde and xylene in NASA tests.
benzene and trichloroethylene; pet-safe.
benzene and formaldehyde; low-light tolerant.
airborne mold and formaldehyde reduction.
the NASA champ for formaldehyde removal.

themes.title: Bedroom Allies
themes.description: Plants that continue oxygen release or promote relaxation at night.
themes.intro: Most plants take in oxygen and release CO₂ at night. A few don't — CAM photosynthesis lets snake plants and aloe release oxygen overnight, exactly when you're breathing it. Add lavender and jasmine for natural bedside calm.

plants.reason: CAM plant that releases oxygen overnight.
succulent CAM night-oxygen; gel for burns bedside.
scent lowers heart rate and aids sleep.
floral aroma studied for anxiolytic effect.
humidity boost good for sinuses and snoring.
non-toxic, formaldehyde removal while you sleep.
tolerates bedroom shade and night neglect.
large leaves trap airborne particulates.
scent linked to improved sleep memory consolidation.
tall, quiet, removes VOCs.

themes.title: Office Buddies
themes.description: Low-maintenance plants for desks and fluorescent-lit offices.
themes.intro: Fluorescent light, dry conditioned air, weekend droughts, occasional flickering attention — most plants would die in an office. ZZ plant, snake plant, pothos, and parlor palm have built careers tolerating exactly that kind of neglect.

plants.reason: tolerates weeks of neglect and low light.
fluorescent-light friendly, waters monthly.
thrives on desks with minimal attention.
easy, pet-safe, produces charming pups.
colorful foliage for low-light cubicles.
Victorian-tough, desk-sized palm.
trailing, forgives missed watering.
compact, tolerates fluorescent light.
unique foliage, easy propagation gifts for colleagues.
glossy statement plant, office-air friendly.

themes.title: Low-Light Champs
themes.description: Plants that genuinely thrive (not just survive) in dim corners.
themes.intro: Not 'tolerate' — actually thrive. Cast Iron Plant earned its name in Victorian gaslit hallways. ZZ plants come from East African understory. This collection gathers species that genuinely flourish where most others fade or yellow out.

plants.reason: Victorian dark-hallway classic.
tolerates near-zero light for months.
handles low light and long drought together.
bred for shopping malls and office shade.
grows in windowless stairwells.
shade-adapted Amazon understory.
blooms even in north-window light.
named for Victorian gaslight parlors.
indestructible mid-century mall planting.
rainforest understory specialist.

themes.title: Deadly Beauty
themes.description: Gorgeous plants that can kill.
themes.intro: Some of the loveliest plants in horticulture will kill you. Oleander stops hearts. Foxglove stops hearts. Ricin from castor bean is the most lethal plant toxin known. This collection is for educated gardeners — children far away.

plants.reason: every part lethal; a single ingested leaf can stop a heart.
cardiac glycosides; historical poison of choice.
aconitine, "queen of poisons"; touched-skin absorption.
seeds contain ricin, among the most lethal biological toxins.
taxine alkaloids stop the heart; deliberate poisonings in antiquity.
tropane alkaloids cause hallucination and death.
tropane alkaloid poisoning; "teenage experimentation" fatalities.
every part lethal to cats; kidney failure.
cardiac glycosides rivaling foxglove.
bulbs deadly if mistaken for onion.

themes.title: Toxic Twins
themes.description: Plants that look tame but hide real toxicity.
themes.intro: Plants that look harmless but hide real danger. Hydrangea leaves contain cyanide. Monstera and peace lily store calcium oxalate crystals. Tomato leaves are toxic to dogs. Common enough to grow in living rooms — quietly dangerous.

plants.reason: cyanogenic glycosides in flowers and leaves.
grayanotoxins affect heart and nerves.
calcium oxalate crystals burn mouth and throat.
insoluble oxalates; cat and dog emergency-vet standard.
oxalate crystals — despite its innocent name.
crystal oxalates cause throat paralysis.
leaves, stems, and green fruit toxic to pets (solanine).
cyanogenic hedge plant; fatal livestock poisonings.
the bright red aril is sweet but the seed inside is deadly.
ubiquitous hedge; dogs die from eating fallen berries.

themes.title: Thorny Defenders
themes.description: Armed plants for security hedges and intruder-proof borders.
themes.intro: Before chain-link fences and security cameras, gardeners used plants. Pyracantha covered burglar walls. Osage orange fenced cattle on the Great Plains before barbed wire existed. This collection arms boundaries with thorns and prickles that hurt.

plants.reason: dense thorns plus salt-tolerant seaside hedge.
the classic "burglar-proof" wall plant.
spiny leaves and evergreen mass.
dense fine thorns.
the hedgerow thorn of rural Britain.
introduced in the 1930s specifically as "living fence."
the original Great Plains cattle hedge before barbed wire.
wicked 2-inch thorns, hardy to zone 5.
the living fence of the Mediterranean and Mexico.
terminal spines on leaves, impenetrable.

themes.title: Hummingbird Magnets
themes.description: Reliable red and tubular flowers for hummingbird gardens.
themes.intro: Hummingbirds eat nearly their body weight in nectar daily, hovering at any tubular red or orange flower. Plant scarlet bee balm, coral honeysuckle, and cardinal flower for a daily airshow from May through October — no feeder required.

plants.reason: hummingbird standard of the Eastern US.
evergreen native vine, hummer favorite.
reblooming red trumpets, long season.
deep cobalt tubes, hummer magnet July–October.
early spring hummer flower.
streamside red trumpets; hummers stitch between flowers.
Southwest hummer native (library has related spp).
patio-friendly tropical hummer attractor.
delicate annual climber loaded with hummer-scale blooms.
Gulf-Coast hummingbird staple.

themes.title: Butterfly Havens
themes.description: Host and nectar plants for monarchs, swallowtails, and friends.
themes.intro: Monarchs need milkweed, black swallowtails need parsley, fritillaries need violets. This collection covers both halves of butterfly gardening — host plants for caterpillars and nectar plants for adults — turning a yard into a butterfly nursery.

plants.reason: monarch host plant and brilliant orange nectar.
wetland monarch host.
so potent it's literally named for them.
swallowtail and fritillary nectar.
monarch migration fuel in late summer.
easiest butterfly-friendly annual, all-summer bloom.
nonstop nectar in southern climates.
monarch and swallowtail late-summer banquet.
airy purple landing pads for fritillaries.
host plant for black swallowtail caterpillars.

themes.title: Bee Banquets
themes.description: Top plants for native and honey bees.
themes.intro: Native bumblebees, mason bees, leafcutter bees, and honeybees each prefer slightly different blooms. This collection lays out a season-long bee buffet — early lavender, midsummer coneflower, the critical late-season goldenrod and aster pollen flow.

plants.reason: early-summer honeybee magnet.
Mediterranean-style bee buffet.
native bumblebee favorite.
broadly attractive native.
critical late-season pollen.
fall honey producer.
long-bloom bumblebee-friendly perennial.
fragrant bee magnet all summer.
when flowered, a honeybee highway.
the highest-nectar annual trialed for bees.

themes.title: Drought Warriors
themes.description: Xeriscape plants for water-restricted yards.
themes.intro: Climate adapts faster than gardens. These xeriscape stalwarts — agaves, lavandins, white sage, prickly pear, manzanita — evolved for summer-dry Mediterranean and Southwestern climates and ask only that you stop watering them out of love.

plants.reason: Southwestern icon; weeks without water.
Mediterranean dry-garden staple.
California chaparral native, summer-dry adapted.
woody Mediterranean; zero supplemental water in zone 8+.
CAM succulent rock-garden base.
Mexican desert classic.
prairie-adapted, taproot survives drought.
California native, fatal to summer-water.
California chaparral aristocrat.
literal desert cactus.

themes.title: Shade Savers
themes.description: Deep-shade performers for north walls and under trees.
themes.intro: Deep shade — under maples, beside north walls — is where most flowering plants give up. Hostas and astilbes don't. Hellebores bloom in February shadows. Solomon's seal turns dappled understories into deliberate woodland gardens.

plants.reason: the king of shade foliage.
shade plumes in moist soil.
partial-shade native blooming shrub.
woodland groundcover native.
dry-shade tolerant.
winter-to-spring shade blooms.
deep-shade evergreen indoor/outdoor.
graceful arching shade perennial.
classic dappled-shade spring beauty.
silver-spotted shade foliage + early blue flowers.

themes.title: Kitchen Herbs
themes.description: Windowsill and bed-side herbs for daily cooking.
themes.intro: The herbs every cook reaches for daily — basil for tomatoes, rosemary for lamb, thyme for poultry, sage for stuffing, mint for tea, parsley for everything. Each grows happily on a sunny sill or balcony pot.

plants.reason: Italian and Thai cuisine centerpiece.
meat roast standard.
Thanksgiving turkey herb.
bouquet garni workhorse.
pizza herb; best dried.
mojito and tea mint.
universal garnish and chimichurri base.
taco and curry essential.
pickles and salmon.
omelet and garnish standby.

themes.title: Edible Weeds
themes.description: Wild lawn and field weeds that are actually food.
themes.intro: Half of what your lawn fights is edible. Dandelion greens are bitter and vitamin-rich; purslane is tangy and omega-3 dense; lamb's quarters out-spinaches spinach. This collection turns common weeds into wild salad ingredients.

plants.reason: leaves (salad), flowers (wine), roots (coffee).
mild salad green, vitamin-C rich.
omega-3-rich lemony-crunch green.
greens and seeds, both edible.
spinach substitute, higher in calcium.
young leaves edible, seeds are psyllium.
cooked spinach; high iron and protein.
lemony greens, high oxalate — cook first.
tart sorrel-like leaves (moderation).
peppery seedpods, salad green.

themes.title: Lucky Charms
themes.description: Plants traditionally grown for luck, wealth, and prosperity.
themes.intro: Across cultures, certain plants are grown for luck and prosperity. Chinese money plant has coin-round leaves; jade plant is feng-shui's classic wealth pot; lucky bamboo's stalk count blesses by number; tulsi guards the Hindu home.

plants.reason: "friendship and prosperity" — round-coin leaves.
the classic Chinese feng-shui money plant.
braided trunks grown for fortune in East Asian commerce.
feng-shui "wealth by the stalk count."
called "Money Plant" in India, grown for wealth.
the four-leaf clover, Irish luck icon.
Chinese symbol of abundance and good fortune.
sacred Hindu protective plant of the home.
modern "fortune plant" in East Asian offices.
Buddhist symbol of prosperity rising from mud.

themes.title: Gothic Garden
themes.description: Dark, dramatic, and mysterious plants for moody borders.
themes.intro: Black pansies, near-black aeoniums, plum-black hellebores, ink-dark hollyhocks. This collection darkens borders with deliberate moodiness — black bamboo's literal black culms, mondo grass like spilled ink. A gothic garden is restraint with menace.

plants.reason: near-black succulent rosettes.
deeply dissected near-black foliage (library has close kin).
darkest purple-black spire.
sinister double blooms.
the iconic blackest garden tulip (via Tulipa gesneriana).
plum-black winter blooms.
almost-black small flower.
literal black stems at maturity.
strap leaves like spilled ink.
white-webbed rosettes, literal spider-plant.
